title: Unit 9 Checkpoint Quiz — Common Expressions & Idioms

options: Agreement, excitement, or encouragement — a very versatile Mexican expression
questions.explanation: '¡Órale!' is one of the most versatile words in Mexican Spanish. It can mean okay, let's go, wow, alright, or come on — context determines the exact meaning.

questions.prompt: What does 'Es muy buena onda' mean about a person?
options: He/She is really cool / chill / has good vibes
questions.explanation: 'Buena onda' literally means 'good wave/vibe'. Describing someone as 'buena onda' means they're cool, laid-back, and easy to be around.

options: To put your foot in your mouth / to make a social blunder
questions.explanation: 'Meter la pata' literally means 'to put your paw in it'. It's used when someone says or does something embarrassing or socially awkward.

questions.prompt: Someone responds to your invitation with 'Ahí viendo...' You should expect:
options: They probably won't come — it's a polite indirect no.
They are very excited to come.
questions.explanation: Mexican communication style often avoids a direct 'no'. 'Ahí viendo' is a face-saving way to decline without saying no outright.
'Nel' is Mexican slang for 'no'. Its counterpart is 'simón' (yes). Both are very casual and common in informal speech.

questions.prompt: How do you express 'I can't stand her' in Mexican Spanish?
questions.explanation: 'Me cae gorda/gordo' means 'I can't stand him/her'. The opposite is 'me cae bien' (I like him/her). Both use the idiom of something 'falling' on you.

questions.prompt: What does 'Ya estuvo' signal in a conversation?
options: That something is finished, settled, or over
questions.explanation: 'Ya estuvo' (literally 'it already was') is used to wrap things up — it signals completion, closure, or the end of a matter.

questions.prompt: In Mexican culture, if someone says 'Estuvo bien...' when asked about food, it likely means:
options: It was okay — possibly a polite way of saying it wasn't great
They loved it and want more
questions.explanation: Mexican communication tends to be indirect. 'Estuvo bien' (it was fine) when asked about food is often a diplomatic understatement — the real enthusiasm would sound like '¡Estuvo buenísimo!' or '¡Qué rico!'
